annotations.label: Proof theory

anchor.snippet: Proof theory is a major branch

annotations.note: Mathlib does not contain a meta-mathematical definition of proof theory itself.

annotations.label: Structural proof theory

anchor.snippet: Structural proof theory is the subdiscipline of proof theory that studies the specifics of proof calculi

annotations.note: No development of structural proof theory or proof calculi appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cut-elimination property

anchor.snippet: a proof calculus has the cut-elimination property if it has the cut rule, but any provable sequent is provable without the cut rule

annotations.note: Grep for cut-elimination in Mathlib returns no results.

annotations.label: Corollaries of cut-elimination (midsequent, Craig interpolation, Herbrand)

anchor.snippet: Gentzen's midsequent theorem , the Craig interpolation theorem , and Herbrand's theorem follow as corollaries of the cut-elimination property

annotations.note: Herbrand's, Craig interpolation, and midsequent theorems are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Subformula property

anchor.snippet: A proof has the subformula property if every of its formula is a subformula of its endsequent

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formal sequent calculus or subformula property.

annotations.label: Subformula property implies consistency

anchor.snippet: A proof calculus with the subformula property is consistent

annotations.note: Depends on infrastructure (sequent calculus, subformula property) that Mathlib does not have.

annotations.label: Analytic proofs as normal forms (natural deduction)

anchor.snippet: we say the analytic proofs are the normal forms

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize natural deduction or normalization of proofs.

annotations.label: Focused proofs

anchor.snippet: A particular family of analytic proofs arising in reductive logic are focused proofs

annotations.note: Focusing in reductive logic is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Harmony of introduction/elimination rules

anchor.snippet: The pair of rules is said to be in harmony if in any proof, maximal formulas can be eliminated by normalizing the proof

annotations.note: Proof-theoretic harmony is not addressed in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Maximal formula

anchor.snippet: A maximal formula is a formula that is introduced, then eliminated later

annotations.note: Maximal formulas in natural-deduction proofs are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Non-local !-rule in linear logic

anchor.snippet: For example, consider the !-rule in linear logic

annotations.note: Linear logic and its exponentials are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Curry–Howard correspondence

anchor.snippet: Structural proof theory is connected to type theory by means of the Curry–Howard correspondence

annotations.note: No file in Mathlib mentions the Curry-Howard correspondence as a formal theorem.

annotations.label: Ordinal analysis

anchor.snippet: Ordinal analysis is a powerful technique for providing combinatorial consistency proofs for subsystems of arithmetic, analysis, and set theory

annotations.note: Ordinal analysis as a metamathematical program is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Well-foundedness implies consistency of T

anchor.snippet: one can prove in finitistic arithmetic that the well-foundedness of a certain transfinite ordinal implies the consistency of T

annotations.note: Finitistic consistency proofs of formal theories are outside Mathlib's scope.

annotations.label: Gentzen's consistency of Peano Arithmetic

anchor.snippet: Ordinal analysis was originated by Gentzen, who proved the consistency of Peano Arithmetic using transfinite induction

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize Peano Arithmetic or its consistency proof.

annotations.label: Takeuti's consistency of Π¹₁-CA₀

anchor.snippet: The first breakthrough in this direction was Takeuti's proof of the consistency of

annotations.note: Subsystems of second-order arithmetic and their consistency results are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Provability logic

anchor.snippet: Provability logic is a modal logic , in which the box operator is interpreted as 'it is provable that'

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize modal logic GL or provability semantics.

annotations.label: Löb's theorem

anchor.snippet: Löb's theorem (if it is provable that the provability of A implies A, then A is provable)

annotations.note: Löb's theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Solovay's completeness theorem for GL

anchor.snippet: Robert Solovay proved that the modal logic GL is complete with respect to Peano Arithmetic

annotations.note: Solovay's arithmetical completeness theorem is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reverse mathematics

anchor.snippet: Reverse mathematics is a program in mathematical logic that seeks to determine which axioms are required to prove theorems of mathematics

annotations.note: Reverse mathematics as a program is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Axiom of choice and Zorn's lemma equivalence

anchor.snippet: the classical theorem that the axiom of choice and Zorn's lemma are equivalent over ZF set theory

annotations.note: Mathlib proves Zorn's lemma assuming classical choice but does not formalize the ZF-level equivalence between AC and Zorn's lemma.

annotations.label: Bounded sequence has supremum

anchor.snippet: Every bounded sequence of real numbers has a supremum

annotations.note: `Real.exists_isLUB` shows every nonempty bounded-above set of reals has a least upper bound.

annotations.label: Reversal in reverse mathematics

anchor.snippet: The second proof, known as a reversal , shows that

annotations.note: The notion of reversal over a base theory is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Robustness of the Big Five

anchor.snippet: Nearly every theorem of ordinary mathematics that has been reverse mathematically analyzed has been proven equivalent to one of these five systems

annotations.note: The Big Five subsystems of second-order arithmetic are not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Functional interpretations

anchor.snippet: Functional interpretations are interpretations of non-constructive theories in functional ones

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize functional interpretations of formal theories.

annotations.label: Dialectica interpretation

anchor.snippet: The study of functional interpretations began with Kurt Gödel's interpretation of intuitionistic arithmetic in a quantifier-free theory of functionals of finite type

annotations.note: Mathlib defines the Dialectica category `Dial` as a categorical model, but not Gödel's functional interpretation of intuitionistic arithmetic itself.
